title: Chapter 13: The Bug
content: Three days. Seventy-two hours of Leo's face lit blue-white by monitors, running the contract through decompilers like a body through an autopsy. IDA Pro. Ghidra. Binary Ninja. He's thrown six different reverse-engineering tools at this thing and each one spits out something different, something wrong, like trying to translate a language that keeps changing its own grammar mid-sentence.

I've been watching. That's all I can do, really — I don't read code. I tried, once, on day one, and my eyes slid off the syntax like water off glass. It's not my language. But watching Leo work is its own kind of education. The way his fingers move across the keyboard, confident and fast, then stop. Freeze. Backspace slowly. Type again. The rhythm of a mind processing something that doesn't want to be processed.

He hasn't slept. I know because I haven't either. My penthouse has become a war room that doesn't know it's at war yet — monitors on every surface, takeout containers stacked like brutalist architecture, the faint electrical hum of too many machines running too long. The air tastes stale, recycled, like breathing inside a server rack.
"Chloe." His voice is cracked. He doesn't look up. "You need to see this."

I cross the room. My legs are stiff, sore — I've been sitting cross-legged on the floor surrounded by printed-out contract sections, highlighters bleeding through the pages like wounds. The wall behind me is covered in my handwriting. Lines and arrows and question marks. A constellation map of a document that refuses to make sense.
Until now.
"Look at this," Leo says, and taps his screen.
I look. Code. Obviously. But something's different — he's highlighted a string of text buried in the compiled output, and it's not variables or hex addresses. It's words. Human words.
> **Uniform Commercial Code, 1923 Edition**
I blink. Read it again. The glow of the monitor is steady, indifferent, but my pulse kicks up.
"What am I looking at?"

"The contract." He leans back, and I can see the exhaustion written in the hollows of his face, the dark circles, the way his jaw is set. But underneath all of it — underneath the depletion and the strain — there's something else. Something sharp. "It's not just a legal document, Chloe. It's a living program. It executes. It updates. It enforces itself. When you hit 'I Agree,' it compiles. When you break a term, it triggers a penalty function. It's software."
"Okay." My mouth is dry. I reach for my coffee — cold, bitter, the milk gone slightly sour — and sip it anyway. I need the caffeine more than I need the taste. "So what's the 1923 thing?"

"That's the thing." He pulls up another window. This one shows the decompiled structure in a tree format — nested layers, like geological strata. "The contract inherits from older code. Like, way older. The most recent layer is from... I don't know, it updates constantly. But the foundational layer — the root logic everything else is built on — is frozen. Hard-coded. And this—" He points to the string. "This is the legal framework that root logic references. The contract was built to run on an operating system called the Uniform Commercial Code, 1923 Edition."
A hundred-year-old operating system.

I stare at the screen and the screen stares back, and somewhere in my visual cortex a pattern starts forming. Not in the code — I still can't read that. In the structure. The way the layers sit on top of each other, the old buried under the new, the foundational layer invisible unless you dig.
"Oh my God," I say.
Leo looks at me. "What?"
"It's like fresco painting."
He blinks. "What?"

"Fresco." I'm talking fast now, the words tumbling out, because my brain is somewhere my brain hasn't been in months — alive, engaged, *on*. "In art history. Renaissance frescoes. They'd paint directly onto wet plaster, right? And if they messed up, or if they wanted to update the image, they couldn't erase it — they'd plaster over the whole thing and paint a new layer on top. But the old painting was still there. Still underneath. And over time — centuries — the plaster would crack, and you'd start seeing the original showing through. The older image bleeding into the new one. Art historians call them pentimenti. Regrets. The painting's ghost."
Leo's staring at me like I've started speaking in tongues, but I can see the gears turning behind his eyes.
"Chloe, I don't—"
"Show me the contract again. The newest version."

He pulls it up. I lean in, and I'm not reading the code — I'm reading the *shape* of it. The way the indentations nest. The way certain sections are denser, more compact, older. The way others branch out, newer, more complex. It's visual. It's all visual.
"There." I point. "See that clause? It's referencing a jurisdiction — I can tell because the variable naming convention changes at that depth. What jurisdiction?"
Leo scrolls. "Damn. That's... okay, that references the New York State Commercial Arbitration Board."
"Does that still exist?"
He types. The keystrokes are fast, urgent. The hum of the monitors seems to get louder, or maybe that's just the blood in my ears.
"No," he says slowly. "It was dissolved in 1954. Absorbed into the state court system."
"There." I point again, at another dense cluster of code. "That one. What framework does it assume?"
More typing. More scrolling. His eyes move left to right, scanning, processing. "This one assumes the Negotiable Instruments Act as the governing framework for debt transfer."
"Is that still law?"
"The Negotiable Instruments Act was *replaced* by the Uniform Commercial Code in 1952. This is referencing a statute that hasn't existed for—" He stops. Turns to look at me. "Chloe."
I'm already there. The pattern is so clear now I can almost see it glowing, the old painting bleeding through the cracks. The fresco's ghost, showing me what's underneath.
"Leo. This contract was written under law that isn't law anymore."

The silence in the room is absolute. The monitors hum. Somewhere in the building, a pipe groans — the old-infrastructural complaint of a Manhattan high-rise. But in this room, in this moment, there's nothing but the two of us and a hundred-year-old secret.

Leo turns back to his screens. His fingers are moving again, faster now, pulling up reference databases, legal archives, version histories. The decompiler output scrolls and scrolls, and he's cross-referencing each foundational clause against — against what? Against reality. Against the actual current state of the law.

"Okay," he says, and his voice has changed. The exhaustion is still there but there's something else riding on top of it now, something electric. "Okay. The contract's core logic is frozen in 1923. Every execution path traces back to that foundational layer. It references commercial statutes that have been superseded — not just once, but dozens of times. Over a hundred years of legal evolution, and this thing is still running on 1923 code."
"Like a building," I say. "Designed to 1923 safety codes. Trying to pass a 2026 inspection."

"Exactly like that." He's nodding, almost to himself. "And here's the bug — modern law *supersedes* contractual frameworks. That's not a maybe. That's a foundational principle of contract law itself. If a contract violates current statutes, the contract is void. Unenforceable. It doesn't matter what the parties agreed to. It doesn't matter if you signed in blood on camera with a notary and three witnesses. Illegal contracts are null and void. Period."
The word hangs in the air. *Void.* Like a hole. Like an exit.
"If we can prove this contract violates 2026 law—" I start.

"Then the whole mechanism becomes illegal. The enforcement clause, the penalty functions, the soul-harvesting—" He stops. Looks at me with an intensity I've never seen from him before. "Chloe, this isn't just your contract. If the foundational logic is the same for all of them, which it should be — it's template-based, the app generates these things from a boilerplate — then *every* contract it's ever generated has the same bug."
Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand contracts.
Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand victims.
A bug in the code that could kill the whole program.
I sit down. Not gracefully — I just kind of fold, like someone cut my strings. The floor is cold through my sweatpants. My hands are shaking. Not from fear this time. From something else entirely.
"Leo."
"Yeah."
"A hunch isn't a strategy."
"I know."

"We need to know *which* current laws create the violations. Not just that the contract references old law — we need chapter and verse. We need the specific statutes that override the contract's foundational logic. We need to be able to point to a law and say, *there — right there — that clause violates that law, and therefore this contract is void.*"

"I know." He leans back in his chair, runs both hands through his hair. It's gotten longer in the three days I've known him — or maybe he just hasn't washed it. "That's not my area. I can tell you what the code references. I can't tell you whether a modern court would agree that the violation is substantive."
I know who can.
The thought arrives like a knife — sudden, precise, and deeply uncomfortable.
My mother.

Evelyn Hart. Copyright lawyer. Twenty-three years at Whitfield & Associates before she made partner, then fifteen more years running her own practice. She's argued before the Second Circuit. She's written amicus briefs for the Supreme Court. She once told me, at a dinner I spent the entire time wanting to leave, that she could spot an unenforceable contract clause the way a sommelier can spot a corked wine — "before I've even finished reading it, Chloe. It's a feeling. An instinct."
She'd recognize this contract's flaws in minutes. Old frameworks painted over, cracks showing through — she'd see it the way I see pentimenti in a Caravaggio.
She'd also want to talk about why I haven't called in two years.
She'd also want to apologize, again, for things I don't want to hear apologies for.
She'd also look at me with those eyes — my eyes, the same grey-green, the same too-light lashes — and I'd feel all the things I've spent two years burying climb back up into my throat like bile.
But this is bigger than my throat.
"I know someone," I say.
Leo waits.
"A copyright lawyer. She's—" I swallow. "She's my mother."
Something shifts in his expression. Not pity — he doesn't do pity, which is one of the reasons I can stand him. More like recognition. He understands that some contacts cost more than others.
"Will she help?"
"She'll want to." The words taste strange. True, but strange. "Whether I'll let her is a different question."
"Will you?"

I think about Marcus Webb. The hollowed professor. The way he scanned my soup with the dead attention of a man who'd forgotten how to taste. I think about eight hundred and forty-seven thousand people, each one of them a Marcus. Each one of them with their own wall of paintings they'll never look at again, their own students they'll never teach, their own soup they'll never savor.

I think about my mother. The last time I saw her — Christmas, two years ago. She'd come to my apartment. I'd let her in because it was Christmas and I was feeling charitable. She'd stood in my living room looking at my paintings with actual tears in her eyes and said, "You're so talented, Chloe. I never told you enough. I'm sorry."
And I'd said, "You should go."
And she'd gone.
"I'll call her," I say.
The decision doesn't land like I expected it to. It doesn't feel like a sacrifice. It doesn't feel like a concession. It feels like the first offensive move I've made since this whole thing started.
Not escape. *Attack.*
Not break the contract. *Invalidate* it.

If we can prove the contract violates 2026 law — any 2026 law — the soul-harvesting mechanism becomes illegal. Not a loophole. Not a hack. *Illegal.* Toothless. Dead code. A demon's signature on a document that a human court would tear up and toss in the trash.
Not just for me. For all of them. For every single one of the eight hundred and forty-seven thousand.
I stand up. My legs hold this time.
"I'm going to fight this."

Leo doesn't look surprised. He leans back in his chair, and his face does something I've never seen it do before — it *relaxes*. The permanent tension in his jaw, the furrow between his eyebrows, the hunched-forward posture of a man who's been waiting for something to start — it all melts. He cracks his knuckles. Both hands. Loud. The sound cracks through the room like a starter pistol.
"Finally," he says.
And just like that, we're in business.

The penthouse desk stretches between us, wide enough for two. Leo claims the left side with his laptops — three monitors now, arranged in a wraparound curve like the cockpit of a very nerdy spaceship. I take the right side with my notebooks and my wall.

Yes, I write by hand. Deal with it. There's something about the physical act of it — the marker squeaking against the glass-white surface of my idea board, the smell of the ink sharp and chemical in my nostrils, the way each letter requires commitment in a way that typing doesn't — that makes the thinking realer. Stickier. Ideas that exist only on a screen feel provisional. Ideas that exist on a wall feel like architecture.
And we are building something.

The contract glows on Leo's center monitor — the full text, decompiled and annotated, scrolling down into digital infinity. His left screen shows the foundational layer, that 1923 stratum, isolated and highlighted. His right screen is open to legal databases, Cornell's Legal Information Institute, the Uniform Commercial Code archive.

My wall is chaos. But it's organized chaos — the kind that makes sense only to the person who made it. Jurisdictions circled in red. Superseded statutes marked with X's. Arrows connecting clauses to their dead references. And in the center, written in black marker three inches tall: **1923 ≠ 2026**.

The hum of the monitors fills the room. Leo's keyboard clicks like rain on a tin roof. My marker squeaks as I add another connection — another crack in the fresco, another ghost of old law bleeding through.
I take a sip of fresh coffee. Hot this time, not cold. Not sour. I made a fresh pot twenty minutes ago because I realized I deserved fresh coffee, and that tiny act of self-care felt revolutionary.

We're not survivors anymore. We're not victims hiding in a penthouse hoping the devil doesn't knock. We're two people with pattern recognition and a hundred-year-old legal flaw, and we are going to take this contract apart piece by piece until there's nothing left but a smoking pile of unenforceable garbage.

I pick up my phone. My mother's contact stares up at me. Two years of silence compacted into a single screen. Her photo — a professional headshot, because of course — looks back at me with those same grey-green eyes.
Tomorrow, I'll call.
Tonight, we plan.

The camera pulls back — or it would, if this were a movie, which it isn't, which is real, which is my actual life — and the frame widens to show the whole room. Two people in a high-rise. A million dollars of equipment. A wall of handwriting. Three hundred thousand lines of decompiled demonic code. And buried at the bottom of all of it, like a fossil in sedimentary rock, the oldest bug in the book:
The law moved on.
The contract didn't.
Now I just need to find exactly where the cracks are — and whether any court on earth will agree that a hundred-year-old legal flaw is enough to kill a deal with the devil.
And I need to know what my mother will say when she sees it.
